*Includes people in prison for new offenses and with sentence lengths that are not less than one year or life sentences.
::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In Alabama, sentences of five years or less have no minimum term to parole-release eligibility. Sentences of more than five and up to 10 years have an initial parole consideration approximately 18 months prior to the minimum release date. Sentences of more than 10 and up to 15 years have an initial parole consideration 30 months prior to the minimum release date.¹
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility In 2020, 0 percent of people in prison were in prison past their parole eligibility year. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 0 percent. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 54 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, 95 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were males.

::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, 41 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
### Offense Types In 2020, 9 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 91 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for public order (33%) and drug (31%) offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Sentence Lengths In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 1 to 1.9 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## 2023 Projection Methodology ATTENTION, ERROR: This state should not be in the tool

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (0 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁴ # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 21 percent, changing from 31,764 in 2010 to 25,032 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 53 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, 91 percent of people in prison were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, 31 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 19 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Sentence Lengths In 2020, 78 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between >=25 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 12 percent, dropping from 13,536 in 2010 to 11,873 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
In 2020, 83 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 7 percent decrease compared to 2010. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 67 percent of prison releases were conditional releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 100 percent of prison releases were White, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, 82 percent of prison releases were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, 35 percent of prison releases were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 40 percent of prison releases were for people incarcerated for drug offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Incarceration ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/rri_infographic_black_Alabama.png){width=1178} ::: ::: In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Alabama. ::: {.cell} ::: ### Gender Gender disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
:::

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender In 2020, females spent 0.9 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Alabama. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by gender in 2020. For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 11 more years in prison on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## How Disparities Are Calculated While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. *States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.* :::
## Endnotes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac, and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Alabama *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal, February 2023), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-02/alabama_doi_report_2_13_22.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-02/alabama_doi_report_2_13_22.pdf).

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., "American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report" (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, pp. 36-43. For offenses with special restrictions, see [pending].

⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.[pending] Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) Program,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program).
